DOWNEY
BRAND
SEYMOUR
& ROHWER
ATTORNEYS

JUDY H. HERSHER

May 21, 1992

555 CAPITOL MALL
10TH FLOOR
SACRAMENTO, CA
95814-4686
TELEPHONE (916) 441-0131
TELECOPIER (916) 441-4201

HAND DELIVERED
John W. Wallace, Esq.
California Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95804-0807

STEPHEN N DOWNEY
(1926-1959)

Re:  Sacramento/Yolo Port District Assessment
     Engineering Contract
     Letter No. 92-278

CLYDE H. BRAND
(1926-1964)

HARRY B. SEYMOUR
(1926-1977)

Dear Mr. Wallace:

     In response to your telephone question, Bruce Henz of
The Spink Corporation advises that, with the exception
of his 1/14th share of the NRS Venture (which owns the
building The Spink Corporation leases), all other
properties listed as held by Spink "team" members are
personal residences. The MacIvers' personal residence
is one contiguous property, but is denoted by two
assessor parcels (note the consecutive number of the
parcels) on which are located the family home and a
horse barn.

     Very truly yours,

     DOWNEY, BRAND, SEYMOUR & ROHWER

     By_________________________
        Judy H. Hersher

cc: Douglas Thompson, Sacramento/Yolo Port District
    Bruce Henz, The Spink Corporation
    James Day, Esq.
    Keith Pershall, Esq.

WEST2 45439

DOWNEY
BRAND
SEYMOUR
& ROHWER

JUDY H. HERSHER

ATTORNEYS

May 19, 1992

555 CAPITOL MALL
107TH FLOOR
SACRAMENTO, CA
95814-4686
TELEPHONE (916) 441-0131
TELECOPY (916) 441-4021

HAND DELIVERED
John W. Wallace, Esq.
California Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95804-0807

STEPHEN W. DOWNEY
(1926-1959)

Re:  Sacramento/Yolo Port District Assessment
Engineering Contract
Letter No. 92-278

CLYDE H. BRAND
(1926-1964)

HARRY B. SEYMOUR
(1926-1977)

Dear Mr. Wallace:

Enclosed please find a "Brief Description of Assessment
District Progress" prepared by The Spink Corporation
for your review.  Also enclosed is a list by name and
assessors parcel number (A.P.N.) of real property
assets held by those on the Spink "team."  Please
advise if you need any further information.

My present understanding is that the information, as
supplemented, is sufficient to trigger the request for
a formal written advice letter from the Fair Political
Practices Commission that, so long as it is based on an
accurate rendition of the facts and the advice given by
the FPPC is followed, offers immunity to the Port
Commissioners and Spink "team" members under the
specified circumstances in the code and regulations.
If this is incorrect, please advise.

Very truly yours,

DOWNEY, BRAND, SEYMOUR & ROHWER

By: [illegible]
Judy H. Hersher

enc: description and real estate holdings
cc: Douglas Thompson, Sacramento/Yolo Port District
    Bruce Henz, The Spink Corporation
    James Day, Esq.
    Keith Pershall, Esq.


EAST-49258

BRIEF DESCRIPTION OF ASSESSMENT DISTRICT PROGRESS

California law permits the formation of assessment districts to finance public improvements.
Assessments are charges levied upon land receiving a direct special benefit from a public
improvement. Property owners may pay their assessments in cash, or bonds may be issued to
finance all or part of the costs of the improvements, plus issuance costs. Principal and interest
payments on the bonds are paid from the annual bond assessment installments collected from
the property owner on the regular tax bill. Included in a 1915 Act bond issue are a reserve fund,
capitalized interest, if needed, attorney's fees, fiscal agent fees, underwriter's discount and the
cost of any assessment engineering services required.

Many public agencies, particularly smaller ones, frequently employ outside consultants to furnish
services for a special assessment proceeding. An engineer may be employed to furnish all or
part of the necessary engineering services, including surveying, engineering, design, preparation
of plans and specifications and inspection of the work during construction.

An assessment engineer is often employed to assist in the preparation of the assessment. His
primary function is to apportion the assessment among all benefitted lots or parcels within the
assessment district upon some proper formula of benefit. He will also prepare any necessary
legal descriptions for the assessment or bonds. In addition, his duties may include appearance
at the hearing on the assessment preparation of the District Boundary Map, preparation of the
assessment roll, assessment diagrams and the Final Engineers Report. The formulation of this
work requires a thoughtful and often time consuming analysis. The determination of "Special
Benefit" is not a repetitive or cookbook process; the structuring of a new district is seldom a
duplication of the process gleaned from a prior districts. In general an outline procedure is as
follows:

DISTRICT MAP - BOUNDARIES

The district map must be prepared on 18" x 26" sheets. Each sheet must show the number of
that particular sheet, the total number of sheets comprising the map, its relation to each adjoining
sheet and have an encompassing border line leaving a 1" blank margin (Section 3110). The
district map should contain a certificate, signed by the clerk of the agency, which evidences the
date and adoption of the resolution or ordinance describing the proposed boundaries of the district
(Section 3111). The map must also contain the name of the jurisdiction and a distinctive
designation, in words or by numbers, of the district (Section 3110).

The map must show the lots, pieces or parcels of land proposed to be assessed and indicate by
a boundary line the extent of the territory included in the proposed district (Section 3110). The
map is not required to show the nature or location of the proposed improvements, although this
may be done if a copy of the map is to be later used as the "diagram".

It is of the utmost importance that the district boundary be fixed with precision. A property owner
has the constitutional right to know whether and how much of his land is to be assessed. A
bondholder is foreclosing a delinquent assessment can foreclose only on the described lot or
parcel within the district. Any uncertainty as to the location of the district boundary may defeat
the rights of the property owner or bondholder and render the proceedings invalid.

?

ASSESSMENT AND DIAGRAM

All of the special assessment acts require the preparation and filing of and a hearing upon a diagram and assessment. None of the special assessment acts set forth any specific formulas requiring the assessment to be spread or apportioned by any method. All of the acts provide that the costs and expenses of the project are to be apportioned in accordance with "benefits" to be received from the project. The formula occasionally is further refined by the establishment of "zones" of benefit with varying rates of assessment or by adjustments for unusual attributes of some parcels.

ENGINEERS REPORT

The report is a composite document containing (a) plans and specifications, (b) a description of any existing facilities to be acquired, (c) an estimate to the cost and incidental expenses, (d) a diagram and (e) a proposed assessment (Section 10204).

SUMMARY

Assessments are not taxes within the meaning of Article XIIIA, Section 4 of the California Constitution, nor are they subject to the one percent maximum ad valorem tax limitation imposed by (Proposition 13) Article XIIIA. Thus, a two-thirds' vote of the qualified electors of the assessment district is not required to levy assessments or issue bonds. In addition, assessments are not subject to the appropriations limits of Article XIII B.

Assessment district finance for the Sacramento Yolo Port District channel deep cleaning project will utilize the Municipal Improvement Act of 1913 (Streets & Highways Code Sections 10000 et seq., the "1913 Act"), which specifies a procedure for establishing assessment districts; and the Improvement Bond Act of 1915 (Streets & Highways Code Sections 8500 et seq., the "1915 Act"), which authorizes the issuance of bonds. 1915 Act bonds are structured as serial or term maturities, or both, representing level debt service payments and are normally issued in denominations of $5,000.00. 1915 Act bonds are callable on any interest payment date; the premium may vary between 5% to no premium at all after five years. Up to two years of interest may be capitalized in the issue along with the reserve fund. The issuing public agency may elect to advance its own funds to pay any delinquent installments until the delinquent parcel(s) can be sold or redeemed. The issuer is under no obligation to do so and such remedies are normally specified in the bond proceedings prior to issuance.

In the event of continuing delinquencies, the issuing agency normally covenants to begin foreclosure proceedings within a specified period of time following the date of delinquency. Any funds advanced, either from the reserve fund or surplus funds, are reimbursed from foreclosure sales.

misc_pen_c3407

DOWNEY
BRAND
SEYMOUR
& ROHWER

JUDY H. HERSHER

ATTORNEYS

May 18, 1992

555 CAPITOL MALL
10TH FLOOR
SACRAMENTO, CA
95814-4686
TELEPHONE (916) 441-0131
TELECOPIER (916) 441-4021

HAND DELIVERED
John W. Wallace, Esq.
California Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95804-0807

STEPHEN W DOWNEY
(1926-1959)

Re: Sacramento/Yolo Port District Assessment
Engineering Contract
Letter No. 92-278

CLYDE H BRAND
(1926-1964)

HARRY B SEYMOUR
(1926-1977)

Dear Mr. Wallace:

We appreciate the opportunity to supplement our May 6,
1992 letter with the following information.

As I stated on the phone, Spink has authorized this
firm to represent that it also seeks the FPPC's formal
written advice on the matters detailed in my letter of
May 6, 1992 to the FPPC. As the Spink bid proposal
under Tab D indicates, the persons who will be working
on the project include Bruce Henz, a registered civil
engineer and principal with Spink; William MacIver, a
registered civil engineer and a senior associate with
Spink; Lloyd Bakan, a principal with the firm
responsible for community relations and public
communication; David H. Werner, Computer Systems
Director; Craig Wecker, a registered land surveyor and
principal with Spink; Bob Ness, a registered land
surveyor and associate with Spink; Mark Smith, a
registered civil engineer and associate with Spink; and
Tom Wildemann, engineer.

None of the above, including the principals, have an
ownership interest in the firm of over 10 percent. The
list of real property assets held by the Spink team and
their immediate families will be sent over to you as
soon as I receive this information. Spink also is
preparing a more detailed explanation of "assessment"
engineering" so that the FPPC can better evaluate the
issues presented.

The names of the PORT Commissioners on whose behalf the
written advice is sought are: Alfonso Z. Gonzalez,
Chair; Willie A. Bell, Vice Chair; Thomas J. Hammer,
Jr., Secretary; James W. Cameron, Jr., Commissioner;

DOWNEY
BRAND
SEYMOUR
& ROHWER

John W. Wallace, Esq.
May 18, 1992
Page 2

Stan Hazelroth, Commissioner; and Obie Brandon,
Commissioner. Gonzalez, Cameron, and Hazelroth will be
part of the PORT "team" detailed in the May 6th letter.
The May 6th letter stated that two PORT Commissioners
would be on the team. With the recent appointment of
two additional PORT Commissioners, the number may
increase by one, or two of the three will be in
attendance at any particular "team" meeting.

Attached to this letter is a map outlining the current
boundaries of the Sacramento/Yolo Port District. The
District includes all of Sacramento County and what
was--before the incorporation of the City of West
Sacramento--all land within the First Supervisorial
District of Yolo County. According to the 1990 Census,
the population of Sacramento County is 1,041,219. A
more recent estimate done by the California Department
of Finance as of January 1992, puts the figure at
1,099,100. The later information was obtained from the
Sacramento Area Council of Government's Data Center
(916/457-2264). The population of the 1st
Supervisorial District of Yolo County, again per the
1990 Census, is 25,611. This information was obtained
from Paula Cooper at the Yolo County Board of
Supervisors (916/372-4309, ext. 8195). Thus, the
current estimated combined population within the
existing Sacramento/Yolo Port District is 1,124,711. It
is anticipated that the boundaries of any new
assessment district will be coextensive with the
current boundaries of the PORT district.

I appreciate your attention and courtesy in this
matter. Please call with any additional questions.

Very truly yours,

DOWNEY, BRAND, SEYMOUR & ROHWER

By [illegible]
Judy H. Hersher

cc: Douglas Thompson, Sacramento/Yolo Port District
Bruce Henz, The Spiak Corporation
James Day, Esq.
Keith Pershall, Esq.
WEST2-45238

DOWNEY
BRAND
SEYMOUR
& ROHWER

FPPC
MAY 6 1992

ATTORNEYS

JUDY H. HERSHER

555 CAPITOL MALL
10TH FLOOR
SACRAMENTO, CA
95814-4686
TELEPHONE (916) 441-0131
TELECOPER (916) 441-4021

May 6, 1992

HAND DELIVERED WITH ATTACHMENTS

STEPHEN W. DOWNEY
(1926-1959)

Scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel
California Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

CLYDE H. BRAND
(1926-1964)

HARRY B. SEYMOUR
(1926-1977)

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

This firm represents the Sacramento-Yolo Port District
("PORT"). On their behalf, we request a formal written
advice letter pursuant to the provisions of Government
Code Section 83114(b). The material facts and
questions follow. Due to the time constraints
associated with the public project described below, we
would appreciate your immediate attention. Time is of
the essence.

MATERIAL FACTS

The PORT is governed by a seven member Board of
Commissioners who hold office for four year terms.¹ A
professional staff run the day-to-day operations and
act as staff to the Commissioners.

On January 3, 1992, the PORT requested a bid proposal
(RFP) from qualified engineers to act as assessment
engineer for the formation of an assessment district to
raise funds for the Channel Deepening Project
("Project").

The Project is a federally authorized and engineered
project to deepen the channel to the PORT. It will be
constructed by the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers (The

________________
¹ Two commissioners are appointed by the Sacramento City
Council; two by the Sacramento County Board of Supervisors; one
by the Yolo County Board of Supervisors;one by the City of West
Sacramento; and one is appointed jointly by the City and County
of Sacramento.

DOWNEY
BRAND
SEYMOUR
& ROHWER

California Fair Political Practices Commission
May 6, 1992
Page 2

"Corps"). Under an existing agreement between the
Corps and the PORT, the PORT must share in all costs,
as well as provide the necessary land, easement and
rights of way. The Project is currently on hold to
enable the PORT to raise the necessary funds for
completion. The Project will permit larger ships to
reach the PORT, thereby increasing trade through the
PORT, and enhancing the economic base of both
Sacramento and Yolo County.

The PORT's recent RFP specifically called for bids on
services related to the formation and implementation of
a special assessment district that would spread the
cost of the Project over an as yet undetermined
geographic area. A copy of the RFP is attached as
Exhibit A. Please note the following language in the
RFP:

For the purposes of avoiding any conflict of
interest, either ethical or in appearance,
the required engineering firm will not
exercise public agency decision-making
authority as defined under SB 833². However,
your firm will be deemed disqualified if you
are currently working for or represent a
District executive employee, a District
Commissioner, or a firm or business in which
one of the above retains substantial control.

The PORT received a number of bids, and has not yet
selected a particular firm. One bid, however, was
submitted by the Spink Corporation ("SPINK") located at
2590 Venture Oaks Way, Sacramento, California 95833-
3288, which the PORT is inclined to accept. A copy of
the SPINK proposal (Exhibit B), and the proposed
contract that would govern the agreement (WORK ORDER
AUTHORIZATION AND AGREEMENT (Exhibit C)), also are
appended under cover of this letter.

Regardless of which firm is selected, the PORT intends
to create a special "team" of persons consisting of its
bond counsel, legal counsel, the Port Director Michael
Vernon, the Assistant Port Director John Sulpizio; the
Finance/Administration Director Douglas Thompson, and
two Port Commissioners.

___________________
² SB 833 is now Government Code Section 87100.1

DOWNEY
BRAND
SEYMOUR
& ROHWER

California Fair Political Practices Commission
May 6, 1992
Page 3

The team will meet with the assessment engineering firm
in order to familiarize it with the needs of the PORT
regarding the Project, provide information regarding
the economics and benefits of enhanced shipping trade,
and to reflect on the legality of various cost
methodologies.

As paragraph 30³ of the proposed SPINK Contract
(Exhibit C) states, it is envisioned that the
assessment engineer will, independent of the control
and direction of the PORT or any member of the Team
(other than normal contract monitoring), provide the
PORT with at least two alternatives on:

(1)  the boundaries of a new assessment district;
     and

(2)  the methodology for spreading the cost of
     development among parcels within the
     alternative boundaries of the new assessment
     district.

The PORT does not want the assessment engineer to
express a preference or recommend one alternative over
the other in regard to (1) and (2) above. The Port
Commissioners will make all decisions regarding (1) and
(2) above after input from its staff and the Port
Commissioners assigned to the team, in consultation
with legal counsel. (See footnote 3.)

_______________________

³ Paragraph 30 reads: Assessment Engineer shall arrive at
conclusions with respect to its rendition of information, advice,
recommendation or counsel independent of the control and
direction of client or an official of client (other than normal
contract monitoring), and shall possess no authority with respect
to any client decision beyond the rendition of information,
advice, recommendation or counsel. Finance group meetings, as
described in the Proposal, shall be solely for the purpose of
obtaining legal advisor input and for gathering information from
and providing information to client staff and Port Commissioner
members of the finance group. Assessment Engineer shall not
solicit and client staff and Port Commissioner members of the
finance group shall not provide or seek to provide direction or
control over the performance of assessment engineering services.
Assessment Engineer shall develop and present to the Port
Commission for its consideration at least two (2) alternative
assessment spread methodologies.

DOWNEY
BRAND
SEYMOUR
& ROHWER

California Fair Political Practices Commission
May 6, 1992
Page 4

After a decision is made on (1) and (2) above, the
assessment engineer will assist in the calculation and
recordation of the final assessments, be available at
public forums or hearings to answer questions on or
describe the selected boundaries and spread
methodology, and prepare any diagrams necessary to
accomplish the above.

ADVICE SOUGHT

The PORT requests the following advice:

(1)  We believe that under the stated facts, SPINK
would not be considered a "consultant" under 2 CCR
18700(a)(2)(A) and (B).  Are we correct?

(2)  If SPINK is not a consultant, must the PORT
undertake an independent, substantive review, including
an independent check of underlying data of each of the
alternatives and their methodologies before selecting
any one?

(3)  If an independent, substantive review is
required, may the PORT's Assistant Director of
Engineering (ADE) undertake the review?  Please note,
the ADE is not a member of the "team" detailed above.
Further, assuming SPINK is not a consultant, our
understanding is that questions 4 through 8 below are
irrelevant.  Do you agree?

(4)  If SPINK is a "consultant," by proposing
several alternatives which the PORT will then review on
its own, is SPINK participating in making, or otherwise
using its official position to influence a government
decision?

ADDITIONAL FACTS

SPINK is a professional consulting engineering and
architectural firm headquartered in Sacramento for over
65 years.  It employs some 180 persons.  A significant
number of its employees reside in the Sacramento/Yolo
counties.  SPINK states in its proposal that it has
addressed local issues for numerous public entities
over the years.  Section D of SPINK's RFP contains the
resumes of those professionals it intends to put on the

DOWNEY
BRAND
SEYMOUR
& ROHWER

California Fair Political Practices Commission
May 6, 1992
Page 5

PORT job. These resumes in turn list a number of
instances in which these persons participated in
assessment engineering for West Sacramento, Galt, Natomas, Sacramento City and County, Placerville,
Antioch and Rancho Cordova. It goes without stating
that in its 65 plus year history, and in particular
over the last 12 months, SPINK has clients throughout
Sacramento and Yolo Counties that have paid it in
excess of $250.00 per parcel, including numerous public
and private entities. SPINK's Conflict of Interest
Statement appears under Tab G. It reads:

G.    CONFLICT OF INTEREST STATEMENT

        The Spink Corporation is a locally owned
        and based multidisciplinary consulting
        engineer, planning and architectural
        firm that has been in business for over
        65 years. We have provided services in
        the field of surveying, land planning,
        architecture and nearly all aspects of
        engineering as required by our clients.

        The fact that we have such a diversity
        of large and small property owners as
        well as public agencies as clients,
        serves to validate that we have always
        been objective in our analysis and
        recommendations on all of our projects.
        We believe that this on-going
        involvement with a broad client base
        makes us more conscious of any potential
        for a conflict of interest than most
        other firms.

        Specifically, we feel there is no
        financial, business interest or
        relationship with any party that would
        effect The Spink Corporation's ability
        to perform work in any manner other than
        that which would be in the best interest
        of the Sacramento-Yolo Port District,
        and we are not currently working for or
        represent a district executive employee,
        a district commissioner, or a firm or
        business in which one of the above
        retains a substantial control.

DOWNEY
BRAND
SEYMOUR
& ROHWER

California Fair Political Practices Commission
May 6, 1992
Page 6

At Tab H., SPINK agrees to notify the PORT of any
potential conflict of interest that may arise in the
future due to its assignment as an assessment engineer.

ADDITIONAL ADVICE SOUGHT

(5) Under the above additional facts, assuming
SPINK is a consultant, does it have an economic
interest in any decision made by the PORT?

(6) Under the additional facts stated above, is
it reasonably foreseeable that the decision rendered by
the PORT will have a financial effect on SPINK's
principals and employees and/or their immediate
families?

(7) Under the additional facts stated above, will
the foreseeable effect be material?

(8) SPINK has been retained in the past by a
diverse and broad-based public and private clientele.
We believe that any methodology recommended by SPINK
would have an indistinguishable effect on its
principals, employees and clientele as on a significant
segment of the public generally. Do you concur?

Thank you for your time and consideration. The PORT
wishes to make an informed decision as soon as
possible. We look forward to your guidance.

Very truly yours,

DOWNEY, BRAND, SEYMOUR & ROHWER

By ___________________________
JUDY H. HERSHER

JHH:ey
WEST2-44343

DOWNEY (1926-1959) Re: Sacramento/Yolo Port District Assessment Engineering Contract Letter No. 92-278 CLYDE H. BRAND (1926-1964) HARRY B. SEYMOUR (1926-1977) Dear Mr. Wallace: Enclosed please find a "Brief Description of Assessment District Progress" prepared by The Spink Corporation for your review. Also enclosed is a list by name and assessors parcel number (A.P.N.) of real property assets held by those on the Spink "team." Please advise if you need any further information. My present understanding is that the information, as supplemented, is sufficient to trigger the request for a formal written advice letter from the Fair Political Practices Commission that, so long as it is based on an accurate rendition of the facts and the advice given by the FPPC is followed, offers immunity to the Port Commissioners and Spink "team" members under the specified circumstances in the code and regulations. If this is incorrect, please advise. Very truly yours, DOWNEY, BRAND, SEYMOUR & ROHWER By: [illegible] Judy H. Hersher enc: description and real estate holdings cc: Douglas Thompson, Sacramento/Yolo Port District Bruce Henz, The Spink Corporation James Day, Esq. Keith Pershall, Esq. EAST-49258 BRIEF DESCRIPTION OF ASSESSMENT DISTRICT PROGRESS California law permits the formation of assessment districts to finance public improvements. Assessments are charges levied upon land receiving a direct special benefit from a public improvement. Property owners may pay their assessments in cash, or bonds may be issued to finance all or part of the costs of the improvements, plus issuance costs. Principal and interest payments on the bonds are paid from the annual bond assessment installments collected from the property owner on the regular tax bill. Included in a 1915
